nodes.name: Shakespeare
nodes.description: Plays, characters, and quotes from Shakespeare

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *Hamlet*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1600–1601; one of Shakespeare's longest plays
Set in Denmark; Prince Hamlet avenges his murdered father by killing his uncle Claudius
Contains more quoted lines than any other Shakespeare play
Source material: the Scandinavian legend of Amleth, via François de Belleforest's *Histoires Tragiques* (1570)
The First Folio (1623) classifies it among the Tragedies

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the soliloquy beginning 'To be, or not to be, that is the question'?
items.explanation:
Act 3, Scene 1 of *Hamlet*; spoken by Prince Hamlet contemplating existence and suicide
Among the most quoted lines in the English language
Three substantially different versions exist across the First Quarto, Second Quarto, and First Folio texts
Laurence Olivier's 1948 film opens with this soliloquy delivered in voice-over
items.cardFront: "To be, or not to be, that is the question"

links.text: "To be, or not to be, that is the question"

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *Macbeth*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1606; Shakespeare's shortest tragedy
Set in medieval Scotland; Macbeth murders King Duncan to seize the throne
Superstition holds that saying the title in a theatre brings bad luck — actors call it 'The Scottish Play'
Based on Raphael Holinshed's *Chronicles of England, Scotland and Ireland* (1577)
Featured in a 2015 film adaptation starring Michael Fassbender and Marion Cotillard

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play contains the speech beginning 'Tomorrow and tomorrow and tomorrow, / Creeps in this petty pace from day to day'?
items.explanation:
Act 5, Scene 5 of *Macbeth*; spoken by Macbeth upon learning of Lady Macbeth's death
Full line continues: '...to the last syllable of recorded time'
William Faulkner took the title *The Sound and the Fury* from the same speech: 'full of sound and fury, signifying nothing'
Expresses nihilistic despair — Macbeth sees life as meaningless after a reign of murder

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *Othello*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1603; set in Venice and Cyprus
Othello, a Moorish general, is manipulated by Iago into believing his wife Desdemona is unfaithful
Source: Cinthio's novella collection *Gli Hecatommithi* (1565)
Oliver Parker's 1995 film starred Laurence Fishburne as Othello — first Black actor in the lead role in a major film adaptation

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play features the villain Iago, who manipulates the protagonist into killing his innocent wife?
items.explanation:
Iago is often called Shakespeare's greatest villain — his motives remain deliberately ambiguous
Claims to be passed over for promotion by Othello in favor of Cassio; possibly also jealous of Othello's marriage
The character has no redemption arc; he refuses to explain his actions at the play's end
Verdi's opera *Otello* (1887) is based on the play

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *King Lear*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1605–1606; based on the legendary pre-Roman British King Leir
Lear divides his kingdom among three daughters based on their professions of love, with catastrophic results
Considered one of Shakespeare's most emotionally devastating works
Nahum Tate's 1681 adaptation gave it a happy ending — this version dominated the stage for 150 years
The Fool disappears mysteriously midway through and is never explained

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *Romeo and Juliet*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1594–1596; among Shakespeare's most performed plays worldwide
Based on Arthur Brooke's narrative poem *The Tragicall Historye of Romeus and Juliet* (1562)
Set in Verona, Italy; the feud between the Montague and Capulet families drives the plot
Musical adaptation: *West Side Story* (1957, then 1961 and 2021 films)
Francesca da Rimini in Dante's *Inferno* is sometimes cited as a precursor to the star-crossed lovers concept

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *A Midsummer Night's Dream*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1595–1596; one of Shakespeare's most fantastical plays
Set in Athens and an enchanted forest ruled by Oberon and Titania, fairy king and queen
The mechanicals (working-class tradesmen) rehearse a play within the play, culminating in 'Pyramus and Thisbe'
Central to the plot: a love potion that causes characters to fall in love with the first creature they see upon waking

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play features Puck (Robin Goodfellow), a mischievous fairy who causes chaos with a love potion?
items.explanation:
Puck is the servant of Oberon, the fairy king
His most famous line: 'Lord, what fools these mortals be!'
Puck's mistaken application of the love potion drives the comedy's central plot
Mendelssohn composed incidental music for the play in 1842, including the famous 'Wedding March'
items.cardFront: Puck / Robin Goodfellow (mischievous fairy, love potion)

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *Much Ado About Nothing*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1598–1599; set in Messina, Sicily
Central couple Beatrice and Benedick are tricked into falling in love through an elaborate scheme
Their witty verbal sparring is one of Shakespeare's most beloved comic dynamics
Kenneth Branagh directed and starred in a celebrated 1993 film adaptation set in Tuscany

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *The Merchant of Venice*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1596–1598; classified as a comedy in the First Folio but often called a 'problem play'
Features the 'pound of flesh' bond and the casket lottery for Portia's hand
Considered Shakespeare's most controversial play due to the portrayal of Shylock
Al Pacino played Shylock in a 2004 film adaptation

items.prompt: Which Shakespeare play features Shylock, a Jewish moneylender who demands a 'pound of flesh' as collateral for a loan?
items.explanation:
Shylock lends money to Antonio on the condition that forfeiture is a pound of Antonio's flesh
The 'Hath not a Jew eyes?' speech (Act 3, Scene 1) is one of Shakespeare's most powerful monologues on shared humanity
Portia famously defeats Shylock's legal claim with the argument that the bond specifies flesh but not blood
Debated as both antisemitic caricature and sympathetic portrait of a persecuted man

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *Twelfth Night*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1601–1602; subtitle 'Or What You Will'
Viola disguises herself as a man (Cesario) after a shipwreck, creating a love triangle with Orsino and Olivia
Features Malvolio, the pompous steward tricked into wearing yellow stockings and cross-garters
Was likely performed at the court of Queen Elizabeth I at the Twelfth Night festivities of 1601 or 1602

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *The Taming of the Shrew*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1590–1592; among Shakespeare's earliest plays
Petruchio woos and 'tames' Katherina (the 'shrew') while Lucentio pursues her sister Bianca
Among Shakespeare's most controversial plays for its depiction of gender roles
Modern adaptation: *10 Things I Hate About You* (1999), set in a high school in Seattle

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *Henry V*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1598–1600; covers the English victory at the Battle of Agincourt (1415)
'Once more unto the breach, dear friends' and 'We few, we happy few, we band of brothers' are from this play
Kenneth Branagh's 1989 film adaptation is considered definitive; he also played Henry in a 1951 Laurence Olivier version
Continues from *Henry IV, Parts 1 and 2*, in which Prince Hal (Henry) is a carousing youth

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *Richard III*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1592–1593; covers Richard's rise to and fall from the English throne
Opens with the famous line: 'Now is the winter of our discontent'
Richard III's skeleton was discovered under a Leicester car park in 2012; confirmed by DNA in 2013
Shakespeare's portrayal is largely Tudor propaganda — depicting Richard as a deformed villain

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *Julius Caesar*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1599; covers the assassination of Caesar and its aftermath
Brutus and Cassius lead the conspirators; Mark Antony's 'Friends, Romans, countrymen' oration turns the crowd
Caesar's death is depicted in Act 3; the play shifts focus to Brutus and Antony for the remainder
Classified as a Tragedy in the First Folio despite its historical subject; sometimes grouped with the Roman plays

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *Antony and Cleopatra*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1606–1607; follows *Julius Caesar* chronologically
Depicts the love affair between Mark Antony and the Egyptian queen Cleopatra VII
One of Shakespeare's longest plays; spans multiple locations across the Roman world and Egypt
Cleopatra's suicide by asp bite ('Give me my robe, put on my crown') is among Shakespeare's most theatrical deaths

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *The Tempest*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1610–1611; believed to be Shakespeare's final solo-authored play
Prospero, the rightful Duke of Milan, uses magic to manipulate events on a remote island
Caliban — Prospero's enslaved servant — is central to postcolonial readings of the play
Shakespeare likely retired to Stratford-upon-Avon shortly after writing it

items.prompt: What genre is Shakespeare's *The Winter's Tale*?
items.explanation:
Written c. 1610–1611; one of the 'late romances' alongside *The Tempest* and *Pericles*
King Leontes's jealous delusion drives the first half (tragic); his daughter Perdita's story resolves it (comic)
Contains the famous stage direction: 'Exit, pursued by a bear'
Hermione's apparent return to life as a statue is one of Shakespeare's most magical theatrical moments
